---
title: ""
output: 
  html_document: 
    theme: cosmo
    css: style.css
---

#

<center> ![A picture of me at work](me25.jpg) </center>

## <span style="background-color: black;">Bob Week</span>

<p align=justify style="background-color:#2E2E2E;
border-radius:4px;
font-size:16px;
padding:15px;
margin:5px;"><b>
I am interested in the relationship between patterns and processes shaping ecological communities. This inquiry leads to a broad range of questions involving the role of contemporary coevolution, the structure of interaction networks, the effects of genetic architectures, the role of geography, feedbacks with abundance and ecosystem processes and the macroevolutionary history of interacting taxa. However, developing models and statistical methods to explore these areas and understand their relative contributions to the structure and dynamics of ecological communities leads to challenging technical problems. By combining tools from stochastic processes, theoretical quantitative genetics and population genetics with classical and Bayesian statistics, I collaborate with evolutionary
biologists, ecologists and mathematicians to study ecological communities and develop model-based approaches to infer ecological and evolutionary processes occurring in the wild.
</b>
</p>

### <span style="background-color: black;">Current Position & Trajectory</span>

<p align=justify style="background-color:#2E2E2E;
border-radius:4px;
font-size:16px;
padding:15px;
margin:5px;"><b>
I am a first-year postdoctoral researcher at [The Bradburd Lab](http://www.genescape.org/) studying continuous-space population genetics. My long-term goal is to start a lab with two primary objectives. The first is to integrate mathematical approaches to genetics, coevolution, community ecology and ecosystem ecology for theoretical explorations of the causes and consequences of genetic and ecological patterns across spatial, temporal and taxonomic scales. The second is the development of statistical methods to infer ecological and evolutionary processes and to predict future states of populations, communities and ecosystems.
</b>
</p>

### <span style="background-color: black;">PhD Work</span>

<p align=justify style="background-color:#2E2E2E;
border-radius:4px;
font-size:16px;
padding:15px;
margin:5px;"><b>
I defended my PhD June 30, 2020. My dissertation contributes to a coevolutionary theory of community ecology with a focus on plant-pollinator networks. Using Diffusion Limits of Measure-Valued Branching Processes (e.g., [super-Brownian motion](http://www.math.ubc.ca/~perkins/superbrownianmotionandcriticalspatialsystems.pdf)), my work provides a stochastic synthesis of quantitative genetics and population ecology ([Week et. al. 2021](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jtbi.2021.110660)). I apply this synthetic approach to derive models of coevolving communities that track the stochastic dynamics of abundance and quantitative traits. Using this framework, I have investigated the evolutionary dissolution of mutualisms experiencing coevolutionary arms races ([Week & Nuismer 2021](https://doi.org/10.1086/714274)) and have developed a Maximum Likelihood method to infer the strength of coevolution between pairs of species using spatially structured phenotypic data ([Week & Nuismer 2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/ele.13231)). My defense was recorded and can be viewed [here](https://youtu.be/HpvkIWx_3Ds). Interactive slides are available [here](https://bobweek.github.io/coevolutionary-community-ecology.html).
</b>
</p>

### <span style="background-color: black;">Publications</span>

<link rel="stylesheet" type="text/css" href="https://cdn.datatables.net/responsive/2.2.5/css/responsive.dataTables.min.css"/>
<script type="text/javascript" src="https://cdn.datatables.net/responsive/2.2.5/js/dataTables.responsive.min.js"></script>

```{r, echo=F, warning=F, class.output="bg-success"}
library(scholar)
library(DT)
pubs = get_publications('vtSczyYAAAAJ')
pubs.smaller = data.frame(pubs$year,pubs$author,pubs$title,pubs$journal,pubs$cites)
colnames(pubs.smaller) = c('Year','Author','Title','Journal','Cites')

datatable(pubs.smaller, rownames = FALSE, extensions = 'Responsive', options = list(dom = 't',
  initComplete = JS("
    function(settings, json) {
      $(this.api().table().header()).css({
        'background-color': '#1F1F1F',
        'color': '#fff'
      });
    }")
)) %>% formatStyle(
  c('Author','Year','Title','Journal','Cites'),
  target = 'row',
  backgroundColor = "#2E2E2E",
  color = "white"
)
```

<noscript>
    <style type="text/css">
        .pagecontainer {display:none;}
    </style>
    <div class="noscriptmsg">
    This website uses javascript to display my publication record. The interactive simulations and components of website navigation also require javascript. Please return using a web browser with javascript enabled to enjoy this content.
    
    -- yours truly
</noscript>
